hero.title:
It all starts
from a shape
hero.sub: A journey from suspension bridges to telescope mirrors, until you discover — on your own — what a parabola really is.

world.label: The parabola in the world
world.title:
This shape is everywhere
around you
world.intro: Before any formula, look: how many times a day do you encounter this curve without knowing it?
world.caption: ↑ Three animated scenes: launch trajectory · suspension bridge · parabolic reflector

bridges.title: Suspension bridges
bridges.text: The main cables of the Golden Gate and almost every suspension bridge trace a parabolic curve, perfect for distributing weight.

mirrors.title: Parabolic mirrors
mirrors.text: Telescopes, satellite dishes and lighthouse reflectors exploit the parabolic shape to concentrate rays and signals at a single point.

trajectories.title: Trajectories
trajectories.text: A kick, a throw, a jump: any object launched into the air follows exactly a parabola.

architecture.title: Architecture
architecture.text: Parabolic arches appear in cathedrals, stadiums and large roof structures: they support enormous loads with structural elegance.

food.title: Even in food
food.text: The cross-section of an ice cream, the curve of a banana, the water jet of a fountain: all parabolas.

antenna.title: TV Antennas
antenna.text: The "dish" on your neighbour's balcony is literally half a parabola: it captures the satellite signal and focuses it at a precise point.

explore.label: Activity 1 & 2
explore.title: Play with these shapes
explore.intro: We won't tell you anything yet. First explore, then the explanation will come by itself.

act1.title: Launch the ball
act1.desc: Change angle and force. Watch the trace left in the air. What shape is it?

act2.title: The antenna and rays
act2.desc: Move the slider to change the depth of the "bowl". Notice where all the parallel rays from above converge.
act2.hint: 💡 All rays meet at the same point. That point is called the focus.

discover.label: Activity 3 & 4
discover.title:
Build the curve
from scratch
discover.intro: You're about to discover for yourself the secret rule that generates this shape.

act3.title: Find the "right" points
act3.desc:
In the plane there is a point F (focus) and a line d (directrix).

Click on the points that seem equidistant ?Equidistant = at the same distance from two different things from both F and line d.
act3.canvasHint: Click where you think dist(P,F) = dist(P,d)
act3.ok: ✅ Correct! That point is truly equidistant from F and d!
act3.ko: ❌ Not quite: the two distances are not equal. Try again!

act4.title: Move the point on the curve
act4.desc: Drag point P along the red curve. Observe the two distances. Do you notice anything?
act4.drag: ↔ Drag P on the canvas above!
act4.equal: ✨ Yes! The two distances are (almost) identical! This holds for EVERY point on the curve.
act4.observe: ↔ Drag P and observe the distances…

def.label: The answer
def.title:
Now you can say it yourself:
what is a parabola?
def.intro: In the previous activities you already discovered it. Here it is, finally, in the language of mathematics.
def.text:
A parabola is the set of all points in the plane that are equidistant from a fixed point called the focus (F) and from a fixed line called the directrix (d).

For every point P on the parabola:
def.form1: Base form — vertical axis, vertex at origin
def.form2: General form — a determines opening and direction
def.form3: Horizontal axis — opens right or left

bonus.title: Play with a, b, c
bonus.desc: Now that you know what a parabola is, explore how the coefficients change the curve.

congrats.title: 🎉 You discovered the parabola!
congrats.text: You started from a bridge and an antenna. You launched balls, measured distances. And now you know what unifies everything: the rule of focus and directrix.
